title: Andrew Carnegie Interview - Part One - YouTube
text:
uh today we're privileged to be here in
the confines of the eure Springs
Carnegie Library to interview one of
America's captains of industry Andrew
Carnegie Mr Carnegie is being portrayed
by Mr Allan Epley a longtime resident of
Eureka Springs a municipal court judge
in Ure springs for over 20 years circuit
court judge for the 19th District East
in Arkansas co-chair of the Carnegie
Centennial committee and vice president
of the library Board of
Trustees Andrew Carnegie was an American
icon and the embodiment of the American
dream an immigrant who made it from Rags
to Riches born in Scotland in
1835 he left with his family at the age
of 12 for the promise of
America during our early years of
development our experiences have an
impact on our beliefs and our values and
how we view the challenges that life
brings our way I think that our viewers
would appreciate it if you spent some
time here telling us about your earliest
memories and how they might have
influenced your
life
um we were extremely poor in Dum
ferling Scotland where I was born
um we lived in a two room house that now
seems impossibly
small my dad William was
a uh linen uh Weaver did find a mask he
was a a skilled Artisan in
weaving and his Loom took up the bottom
floor of our house and um we we lived uh
upstairs and the major piece of
furniture in the upstairs was of course
the bed uh there was barely room to get
around any for anything
else uh I went to school when I was 8
years old in a what uh would be called a
one room Schoolhouse
here uh it had 180 to 190 students at
the time taught by one
instructor and uh I did very well in the
two years that I
attended um well perhaps it was four
from uh I started at 8 and left for the
United States when I was 12 and so uh
during that time I learned uh to uh
write uh my letters and to do Psalms and
um and to read I learned to read and
uh uh then mother uh uh the power loom
came along the steam driven power looms
came along and took away uh the business
it started with the cotton and
soon turned to the linen and uh uh the
jobs dried up for my father my mother
was doing the best she could to uh
support
us um and finally her friend offered us
uh a loan of 20 lbs to help us immigrate
to the United
States that's wonderful uh so you moved
to uh
America and
um and your family settled in alany area
of Pennsylvania outside alany city
aligan city uh was a separate town from
Pittsburg at the time but of course they
were cheek to Gile as we say now uh they
were
uh close neighbors and uh and over the
uh within the period of my life alany
City in essence was U enveloped by
Pittsburgh um when we came to uh to it
took 3 months by the way to cross the
ocean and uh travel up the river by uh
steamboats various uh methods of getting
across the country it took three months
to complete the move to just to move us
physically uh from Scotland to alagan
City yeah and uh uh our aunt had
preceded us to alagan city and had a a
house lodging that she could provide for
us uh
rentree um for the first several years
that we lived here my father again
attempted uh to um make a living with
looms and he would uh weaving and he
would continually
produce items of tablecloth and the like
but there was very little market for
them and uh it was my mother again um
providing for us and at and at age 12 I
went to
work
so um tell us about your early work
experience and where you worked and my
father and I both found employment at
the anchor cotton Mills where I began
work as a bobin boy uh taking bobbins of
U cotton thread to uh the various
machines uh as the as the uh person's
running machine
needed uh my father quit after about two
months yeah and I continued working
there and um brought home the
Magnificent sum of approximately $2.50 a
week but soon after that um the
opportunity came along at another 25 C
raise to work as
a telegraph
Runner would would take the became hired
by another Scotsman to uh work for the
old rally Telegraph company and
um U run from uh the receipt of the
messages to the recipients and and or
wait for a reply and and take it back to
be sent and
um um the greatest thing fear that I had
at that time was learning the streets of
Pittsburgh so I made an effort to
memorize all of the uh the
streets and um during this time
um I also learned to listen to the
Telegraph and eventually learn the the
code to where I could take it by ear
would did not have to take it down other
than to write it
out and um I came to the attention of a
gentleman who for quite some time would
be a uh great benefit to me in my
career
um Mr Thomas
Scott and he worked for the Pennsylvania
Railroad and he hired me when he became
the superintendent of the western
division of the Pennsylvania Railroad in
um Pittsburgh to be his personal
Telegraph
operator
and uh
that led to a long business relationship
with him
he
arranged for me to acquire a uh an
interest in the O'Reilly Telegraph
company
U by uh advising me that for $500 I
could acquire shares of ownership in
that business and uh I took this to my
mother and mother then mortgaged a home
we had just been able to acquire for the
money necessary to make that investment
and that was my first ownership uh
investment and we were astounded to
receive the
first check for
monies that I did not have to earn
